articles.title: This Is the Surprising Reason Why We Blow Out Birthday Candles
articles.content:
我有五个孩子，这意味着我这辈子大概点了又吹、吹了又点过四千根生日蜡烛。我曾在好市多的大张蛋糕上烧焦过头发。我曾看着一个蹒跚学步的孩子把点燃的蜡烛当成面包棒往嘴里塞。我曾站在烟雾弥漫的厨房里，因为那些“整蛊蜡烛”耍了错误的把戏。至于因为各种生日蜡烛引发的兄弟姐妹混战，我调解过多少次就更不用提了。有一次令人难忘的WWE式大战，最后根汁啤酒喷到了天花板上（四年过去了，那痕迹还在），我叹了口气问丈夫：“等等，我们为什么要这样做？谁会觉得给孩子火种放在公共食物上是个好主意？”

原来这个问题真的有答案。“吹灭生日蜡烛的仪式是一个相当近期的、有充分文献记载的传统，”科尔盖特大学历史学家、约翰·D·凯瑟琳·T·麦克阿瑟捐赠主席兼教授夸西·科纳杜博士说，“真正有趣的是这个仪式是如何走向全球的。”

继续阅读，了解我们为什么把火放在甜点上然后立刻用自己的呼吸把它吹灭（还可能把口水喷到蛋糕上）而所有人都在旁边看着——这个真正令人着迷的故事。它涉及古代神灵、德国幼儿、一本瑞士民俗期刊，以及最终——米老鼠。（一切最终都会追溯到迪士尼。）

蜡烛出现在生日蛋糕上是在十八世纪中叶。这有点令人惊讶，因为蜡烛本身是古老的。几千年来，人们一直为了宗教和实用目的点燃灯芯，早在那之前就有人想到要把蜡烛插进糖霜里。

那么在蛋糕和蜡烛成为标准之前，人们是如何庆祝生日的呢？在大部分历史中，如果你要纪念生日，它看起来更像宗教仪式而不是派对。例如，罗马人会为精英阶层举办正式的生日庆祝活动，但“派对”实际上是对一个人“守护神”的供奉——人们相信这种保护性神灵会陪伴一个人一生。这不完全是玩盲人摸象游戏的那种乐趣，但毕竟是庆祝活动。

要理解我们是如何从古代供奉发展到今天的传统，你必须追溯到非常古老、非常人性化的观念——火等于神圣。

在古代文化中——从吠陀世界到波斯、希腊和罗马——火焰被视为神圣存在的替代品。“火作为神圣存在的观念非常古老且跨文化，”流行文化历史学家玛丽·尼古拉解释道，她研究人类社交仪式包括生日的历史。早在有人对着邓肯·海因斯蛋糕跑调唱歌之前，古代家庭和寺庙就在祭坛和仪式中使用火作为一种方式，去触及比自身更伟大的存在。

在这里你会遇到关于生日蜡烛最流行（也最站不住脚）的理论：它们追溯到古希腊，当时人们向月亮女神阿尔忒弥斯供奉一种由类似瑞可塔奶酪、面粉和蜂蜜制成的芝士蛋糕——叫做“安菲芬”，并用点燃的火炬装饰以模拟月光。这是一个很棒的故事。它出现在博物馆说明牌、百科全书条目和许多网络文章中。问题是，这些月亮蛋糕与现代生日之间没有实际的文献联系。

“古希腊或古罗马起源的流行观念在历史记录中没有支持，因此仍然是民间传说，”科纳杜说。尼古拉同意这是一个诱人的联系……但不是一个可证明的联系。“这很合理，”她说。“只是我们目前还没有发现任何历史证据支持它。”

早期基督徒根本不庆祝生日。事实上，早期教会人物积极著文反对庆祝生日，因为那太自我中心了，而且坦白说也太异教了。相反，第一批基督教日历围绕洗礼、复活节和殉道者死亡展开，这些被视为进入天堂的“诞生”。

然而蜡烛从未消失。它们继续存在于基督教礼仪中，用于纪念圣人和标记神圣时刻。蛋糕只是很长时间内没有被邀请参加那个特定的聚会。

蛋糕直到中世纪晚期才作为节日性、非宗教食物重新出现，主要是在富裕家庭。然后，在十七世纪，新教地区开始从圣徒节日转向庆祝个人里程碑——生日、洗礼和婚礼。这一转变为德国奠定了基础，1691年，一位名叫约翰·克里斯托夫·瓦根塞尔尔的学者在关于纽伦堡城的著作中记录了儿童生日蛋糕。没有蜡烛。但这是有记载的最早的生日蛋糕。

蜡烛加蛋糕加生日儿童的特定仪式通常追溯到十八世纪的德国，通过一个叫做“金德费斯特”的儿童节。“在1691年到1740年之间的某个时候，国内创新与象征逻辑相遇，生日蜡烛诞生了，”尼古拉说。她的理论与其说是一个单一的“顿悟时刻”，不如说是旧观念在悄然重新组合。“蛋糕已经是庆祝性的。蜡烛已经意味着生命。儿童已经代表着潜力，”她说。

我们拥有的最早的个人记述之一来自作家约翰·沃尔夫冈·冯·歌德，他描述了1740年代一次庆祝活动中的生日蛋糕，周围环绕着灯光，“像一座祭坛”。这是一个惊人的直观形象，展示了即使在这个小型的家庭仪式远离了真正的宗教仪式之后数代人，它仍然感觉多么神圣。

至于“为什么是德国，为什么是那个时候”：尼古拉指出金德费斯特源于虔敬主义，一种强调真诚奉献并将家庭视为道德训练场的新教运动。“金德费斯特不是为了代祷——而是为了时间、记忆和道德发展，”她说。蜡烛代表着孩子的内心成长，每年点燃一根，外加一根额外的“成长蜡烛”。

到1881年，这个仪式已经传到瑞士，在那里，为《民俗期刊》撰稿的研究人员记录了瑞士中产阶级中蜡烛放在蛋糕上的完整传统——蜡烛在蛋糕被吃掉之前被吹灭。研究世界传统的科纳杜说：“'许个愿然后吹灭蜡烛'仪式的最早文字记载只出现在十九世纪末，描述的是瑞士传统，这可能是从德国传统借用过来的——在德国传统中，这些蜡烛会为生日孩子的精神保护燃烧一整天。”

简短的回答：呼吸。在很长的时间里，跨许多不同的信仰体系，一个人的呼吸基本上被视为与其生命力或灵魂同义。“在许多传统中，包括早期基督教神秘主义，呼吸与灵魂相连，”尼古拉说。“吹灭蜡烛同时默默许愿携带着许愿祈祷的回响——只是在这里，它是家庭的、以孩子为中心的，而且非常世俗化。”

所以当你吹灭蜡烛时，你不仅仅是在对着一些蜡吹气。你正在进行一个小小的、私密的、几乎是祈祷般的行为——没有香和教堂。而且还有糖霜。

一旦金德费斯特在德国存在，它需要被带过大西洋，而德国移民提供了这个途径。“德国移民在十九世纪将它带到美国，然后在二十世纪早期到中期，美国流行文化和战后媒体扩展了它的实践和商业化，使其走向世界，”科纳杜说。费城和密尔沃基等城市的德语报纸在整个1800年代刊登金德费斯特公告，这是历史学家能够追踪这一习俗缓慢进入主流美国生活的方式之一。

到1900年至1920年，蛋糕上的蜡烛在美国和英国都是稳固的中产阶级规范。到1920年代，这个仪式已经完全商业化：蜡烛被大规模生产并以适龄套装销售，通常配有各自的小支架，由大型百货商店销售。

然后出现了可能最终锁定这一传统的时刻：1931年，迪士尼发布了一部以生日派对为中心的短片，它成为数百万家庭在接下来整个世纪里不知不觉地模仿的模板——蛋糕、蜡烛、一首歌和一个愿望，按这个确切顺序。二战后，美国消费文化与世界各地分享了同样的剧本，由旅游、儿童媒体和渴望销售派对用品的品牌推动。

不，地球上并非每种文化都以完全相同的方式吹灭蜡烛和许愿。许多国家有自己的古老的、原生的生日习俗，根本没有单独的蜡烛传统——它们只是在仪式全球化后简单地采用了德国-美国版本。尼古拉指出战后日本是一个明显的例子，指出该国在1950年代已经采用了吹蜡烛的习俗，由美国媒体和品牌推动。同时，其他文化有与蜡烛完全无关的生日仪式——加纳经常以一道叫做“奥托”的红薯早餐菜肴开始一天，俄罗斯人有时用个性化的生日馅饼代替蛋糕，中国则倾向于一碗不切断的“长寿面”。

德国也仍然有自己的做法。不是马上吹灭蜡烛，许多德国家庭使用一个木制“生日花环”，为孩子生命中的每一年点燃一根蜡烛，外加中央一根更高的“生命之灯”，让它们燃烧一整天——这个传统持续到孩子大约十二岁。

根据尼古拉的说法，真正疯狂的是，这一仪式之所以变得如此全球统一，主要是因为媒体，而不是宗教或地理。“流行文化不创造仪式。人创造仪式，”她说。“我们通过几个世纪的渐进仪式化庆祝和行为产生了它。”她说，流行文化所做的是将其标准化为研究人员所谓的“仪式脚本”。正如尼古拉所说：“我们不一定需要被教导任何东西；我们已经知道它是怎么进行的。”

所以下次当你站在昏暗的厨房里，拿着打火机对着一根稍微弯曲的蜡烛，而你身边一个小人正兴奋得浑身发抖时，要知道你不只是在做一个可爱的派对把戏。你是这条链条的最新一环，这条链条贯穿罗马供奉、基督教礼仪、德国虔敬主义和一本瑞士民俗期刊，一直延伸到此刻正放在你柜台上的自制或商店买的蛋糕。

甚至皇室也参与这种盛况的某个版本。伊丽莎白女王 famously 以一年庆祝两次生日，所以，真的，我家那种反复重新点燃蛋糕蜡烛的情况基本上就是皇室级别的传统了。

所以，许个愿。吹灭。需要的话重复，房间里最好有个烟雾探测器。
articles.summary:
I have five kids, which means I have lit, blown out, relit and blown out again approximately 4,000 birthday candles in my lifetime. I have singed my hair over a Costco sheet cake. I have watched a toddler try to eat a lit candle like a breadstick. I have stood in a smoke-filled kitchen because the “trick” candles played the wrong kind of trick. And I won’t even get into the number of fights I’ve had to break up over various birthday-candle sibling shenanigans. After one memorable WWE episode that ended up with root beer on the ceiling (which is still there, four years later), I sighed and asked my husband, “Wait, why do we do this? Who thought giving children fire over communal food was a great birthday idea?”

It turns out that there actually is an answer to that question. “The ritual of blowing out birthday candles is a fairly recent and well-documented tradition,” says Kwasi Konadu, PhD, a historian and the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Endowed Chair and Professor at Colgate University. “What’s really fascinating is how this ritual became global.”

Read on to find out the real, weirdly fascinating story behind why we stick fire on top of dessert and then immediately try to put it out with our own breath (and possibly spray spit over the cake) while everyone watches. It involves ancient gods, German toddlers, a Swiss folklore journal and, eventually, Mickey Mouse. (Everything always ends up back at Disney.)

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Have we always had candles on birthday cakes?

Nope. Candles didn’t show up on birthday cakes until the mid-18th century. That’s a little startling when you consider that candles themselves are ancient. People have been lighting wicks for religious and practical reasons for thousands of years, long before anyone thought to jam one into frosting.

So what did people do to celebrate a birthday before cake and candles became the standard? For most of history, if you were marking a birthday at all, it likely looked more like a religious observance than a party. The Romans, for instance, threw legitimate birthday celebrations for the elite, but the “party” was really an offering made to a person’s genius, a protective spirit believed to walk alongside someone for their whole life. Not exactly pin-the-tail-on-the-donkey type of fun, but celebratory, nonetheless.

What is the history behind birthday candles?

To understand how we got from ancient offerings to today’s tradition, you have to go back to the very old, very human idea that fire equals the divine.

Fire, cake and the gods

Across ancient cultures—from the Vedic world to Persia, Greece and Rome—flame was treated as a stand-in for something sacred. “The idea of fire as divine presence is incredibly old and cross-cultural,” explains pop culture historian Marie Nicola, who researches the history of human social rituals, including birthdays. Long before anyone was singing off-key over a Duncan Hines cake, ancient households and temples used fire in altars and ceremonies as a way to reach for something bigger than themselves.

This is also where you’ll run into the most popular (and most shaky) theory about birthday candles: that they trace back to ancient Greece, where cheesecakes (made with a ricotta-like cheese, flour and honey) called amphiphons were offered to Artemis, the goddess of the moon, and decorated with lit torches to mimic moonlight. It’s a great story. It shows up in museum placards, encyclopedia entries and many online articles. The problem is, there’s no actual documented link between those moon cakes and modern birthdays.

“The popular idea that this began in ancient Greece or Rome has no support in the historical record and thus remains folklore,” Konadu says. Nicola agrees it’s a tempting dot to connect … but not a provable one. “It’s plausible,” she says. “It’s just not supported by any historical evidence that we have found as of yet.”

Then Christianity showed up and rained on the parade

Early Christians didn’t celebrate birthdays at all. In fact, early church figures actively wrote against the celebration of birthdays for being self-focused and, frankly, a little too pagan for comfort. Instead, the first Christian calendars revolved around baptisms, Easter and the deaths of martyrs, which were viewed as a “birth” into heaven.

Candles, though, never went away. They stuck around in Christian liturgy to honor saints and mark sacred moments. Cake just wasn’t invited to that particular party for a very long time.

Cake makes its comeback

Cake didn’t reappear as a festive, nonreligious food until the late medieval period, mostly in wealthy homes. Then, in the 1600s, Protestant regions began shifting away from saints’ feast days and toward celebrating personal milestones instead—birthdays, baptisms and weddings. That shift set the stage for Germany, where, in 1691, a scholar named Johann Christoph Wagenseil documented children’s birthday cakes in his writings on the city of Nuremberg. No candles yet. But it’s the earliest known reference to an actual birthday cake.

So when did we start blowing out birthday candles?

The specific ritual of candles-plus-cake-plus-a-birthday-child is generally traced to 18th-century Germany, through a children’s festival known as Kinderfest. “Somewhere between 1691 and 1740, domestic innovation met symbolic logic, and the birthday candle was born,” Nicola says. Her theory is less about a single “aha” moment and more about old ideas quietly reassembling themselves. “The cake was already celebratory. The candle already meant life. The child already represented potential,” she says.

One of the earliest personal accounts we have comes from the writer Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, who described a birthday cake from a celebration in the 1740s, surrounded by lights “like an altar.” It’s a strikingly literal image of exactly how sacred this little domestic ritual still felt, even generations after it had drifted away from actual religious ceremony.

As for the “why Germany, why then” of it all: Nicola points to Kinderfest’s roots in Pietism, a Protestant movement that emphasized heartfelt devotion and treated the home as a kind of moral training ground. “Kinderfest wasn’t about intercession—it was about time, memory and moral development,” she says. Candles came to represent a child’s inner growth, with one lit for every year of life, plus an extra “one to grow on.”

By 1881, the ritual had traveled to Switzerland, where researchers writing for the Folk-lore Journal documented the full candles-on-a-cake tradition among the Swiss middle class—with candles blown out before the cake was eaten. Konadu, who researches world traditions, says: “The earliest written record of the ‘make a wish and blow out the candles’ ritual only appears in the late 19th century, describing a Swiss tradition, which likely borrowed from a German tradition of burning said candles all day for the birthday child’s spiritual protection.”

How did making a wish get wrapped up in this birthday custom?

Short answer: breath. For a very long time, across many different belief systems, a person’s breath has been treated as basically synonymous with their life force or soul. “In many traditions, including early Christian mysticism, breath was tied to the soul,” Nicola says. “Blowing out a candle while making a silent intention carries echoes of votive prayer—only here, it’s domestic, child-centered and delightfully secular.”

So when you blow out candles, you aren’t just huffing at some wax. You are performing a tiny, private, almost prayer-like act—minus the incense and church. And with frosting.

How did all of this become (ahem) a full-blown tradition?

Once Kinderfest existed in Germany, it needed a ride across the Atlantic, and German immigrants provided one. “German immigrants brought it to the United States in the 19th century, and then in the early to mid-20th century, U.S. pop culture and postwar media expanded its practice and commercialization, making it worldwide,” Konadu says. German-language newspapers in cities like Philadelphia and Milwaukee published Kinderfest announcements throughout the 1800s, which is part of how historians can track the custom’s slow crossover into mainstream American life.

By 1900 to 1920, candles on cakes were a solid middle-class norm in both the U.S. and the U.K. By the 1920s, the ritual had gone fully retail: Candles were mass-produced and sold in age-appropriate sets, often with their own little holders, by big department stores.

Then came the moment that arguably locked in the tradition for good: In 1931, Disney released a short film centered on a birthday party, and it became the template millions of families would unknowingly copy for the rest of the century—cake, candles, a song and a wish, in that exact order. After World War II, American consumer culture shared that same script with the rest of the world, carried along by tourism, kids’ media and brands eager to sell party supplies.

Does everyone now follow this birthday tradition?

No, not every culture on Earth blows out candles and makes a wish in the exact same way we do. Plenty of countries that had their own ancient, homegrown birthday customs didn’t have a separate candle tradition at all—they simply picked up the German-American version once it went global. Nicola points to postwar Japan as a clear example, noting the country had adopted the candle-blowing custom by the 1950s, carried along by American media and brands. Meanwhile, other cultures have birthday rituals that have nothing to do with candles whatsoever—Ghana often starts the day with a sweet-potato breakfast dish called oto, Russians sometimes swap the cake for a personalized birthday pie, and China leans on a bowl of uncut “longevity noodles” instead.

Germany still does its own thing too. Rather than blowing out the candles right away, many German families use a wooden “birthday wreath,” lighting one candle for every year of a child’s life plus a taller “light of life” candle in the center, and let them burn all day—a tradition that continues for kids through about age 12.

What’s really wild, according to Nicola, is that the ritual has become so globally uniform mostly because of media, not religion or geography. “Pop culture doesn’t create the ritual. People create the rituals,” she says. “We generated that over centuries of gradual ritualistic celebrations and behaviors.” What pop culture did, she says, was standardize it into something researchers call a “ritual script.” As Nicola puts it: “We don’t necessarily need to be taught anything; we already know how it runs.”

And now you know!

So the next time you’re standing in a dim kitchen, holding a lighter to a slightly bent candle while a small person vibrates with anticipation next to you, know that you’re not just doing a cute party trick. You’re the latest link in a chain that runs through Roman offerings, Christian liturgy, German Pietism and a Swiss folklore journal, all the way to whatever homemade or store-bought cake is sitting on your counter right now.

Even royalty gets in on a version of this pageantry. Queen Elizabeth famously celebrated two birthdays a year, so really, my household’s multiple-cake-relighting situation is basically monarch-level tradition.

So make your wish. Blow. Repeat as needed, ideally with a smoke detector in the room.

Sources:

  • Kwasi Konadu, PhD, John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Endowed Chair and Professor at Colgate University; personal interview, July 11, 2026
  • Marie Nicola, pop culture historian; personal interview, July 11, 2026
  • National Geographic: “Blowing out birthday candles is surprisingly ancient”

Interesting Facts About Nearly Everything

Tricky Pop Culture Trivia Questions

Trivia Questions to Test Your Smarts

The post This Is the Surprising Reason Why We Blow Out Birthday Candles appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: This Is What the Symbols on Your Food-Storage Container Mean
articles.content:
有时候我会幻想，去商店购物时每样东西只有一种选择会是什么感觉。买一件衬衫时不用纠结它是吸湿排汗面料还是只能手洗，买鸡蛋时不用考虑是农场养殖还是散养的。坦白说，我觉得那样挺惬意的。但遗憾的是，也许是为了更好的选择，我们在决定吃什么食物、用什么产品时，现在有了更多的信息和更多的选择。

以食品储存容器为例。把它翻过来或翻到背面，你很可能会看到一排小符号，这些符号都在告诉你关于容器材质的一些信息，并可能影响你决定是否要使用它。符号有很多种——有些含义明显，有些则比较模糊。这还是说得好听的情况。

所以我们在这里为您提供一份指南，告诉您这些符号的实际含义，指南由退休食品安全和质量专家马丁·布克纳维奇提供。继续往下读，了解详情——并了解为什么这些信息比您想象的更重要。

当涉及您的个人安全时（某些情况下确实如此），听取这些符号告诉您什么就非常重要。忽略其中一些符号可能会使您的食物变得不可食用或食用危险，而忽略另一些符号则可能意味着您错过了一个支持环境保护的选择机会。

但问题来了：尽管潜在的个人和社会风险很高，这些符号并不太一致。除了回收符号——它于1970年创建并被普遍使用，以及食品安全符号——由欧盟创建的国际监管标准——食品储存容器上的其他符号都没有标准化。事实上，大多数符号是由营销部门创建或添加的，以便顾客了解如何最好地使用这些产品。含义相同的符号往往看起来相似，但存在细微差异，让人难以分辨哪个是哪个。

当然，这就是我们发挥作用的地方。

以下是一些最常见的符号，包括它们的样子和含义。提前感谢吧！

**叉子和酒杯符号**

它长什么样：一个酒杯和一个叉子并排放置。

它意味着什么：“它表示产品所用材料被认为可以安全接触食物，”布克纳维奇说。这意味着两个关键事项。首先，制造过程中表面上没有残留任何污染物。其次，如果你按照设计正常使用容器，材料本身不会成为可能转移到食物中的毒素来源。

“当容器看起来不明显是食品容器时，这个符号就很有用了，”布克纳维奇补充道。

**BPA Free符号**

它长什么样：通常只是BPA Free这几个字。有时是BPA外加一个圆圈，中间有一条斜线穿过。

它意味着什么：“这表示容器不含双酚A（BPA），这是一种用于制造某些类型食品容器的化学物质，但已被发现会渗入食物，可能成为健康问题，特别是对婴儿，”布克纳维奇说。

根据梅奥诊所的资料，双酚A暴露可能损害胎儿、婴儿和儿童的大脑，而独立研究表明双酚A与高血压、2型糖尿病和心脏病之间存在联系。换句话说，您和家人经常使用的容器、餐具和水杯中可能不希望含有双酚A。

**微波炉安全符号**

它长什么样：三条水平波浪线，单独放置或在一个看起来像微波炉的方框内。

它意味着什么：这个符号不言自明——它意味着您可以将装有食物的容器安全地放入微波炉进行正常加热。“不仅容器不应融化或破裂，而且容器本身不应有化学物质迁移，”布克纳维奇说。

**冷冻室安全符号**

它长什么样：一个雪花在一个方框内，有时还画有一个小型冷冻室图案。

它意味着什么：容器放入冷冻室时能有效保护食物。“这种材料设计用于承受冷冻温度而不会变脆或破裂，”布克纳维奇说。

**洗碗机安全符号**

它长什么样：一个方框内有底部的餐具和水滴从上方落下。

它意味着什么：简而言之，您可以将容器放入洗碗机。更专业一点来说，这意味着容器可以承受洗碗机的高温和洗涤剂中的任何化学物质，而不会变形或破损。

**可堆肥符号**

它长什么样：带有“可堆肥”或“BPI”字样的树叶，或者一个看起来有点像杯子蛋糕的堆肥堆，周围有箭头环绕。

它意味着什么：“这表示食品包装材料在特定的堆肥环境中会分解成有机物，”布克纳维奇说。根据美国农业部的说法，堆肥是将有机材料回收成可用于丰富土壤和植物的东西的过程。如果您在家庭进行堆肥，这个符号意味着您用完容器后可以将其添加到您的堆肥桶中。

**可生物降解符号**

它长什么样：由两支箭头和一片叶子组成的三角形，或者这个符号在一个圆圈内，写着“可生物降解”或“环保”。

它意味着什么：容器所用的材料最终会分解成简单的天然物质，布克纳维奇解释道。这与可堆肥标志有何不同？标有可堆肥符号的东西需要堆肥环境，而可生物降解符号理论上意味着材料会自行分解。

“这里的问题是，关于时间框架、物品分解的条件以及是否留下任何有问题的材料，都存在疑问，”布克纳维奇说。

**回收符号**

它长什么样：由箭头组成的三角形，中间有一个数字，下面有几个字母。

它意味着什么：这个符号告诉您容器所用塑料的类型是否可以回收。“有些更容易被回收，比如1号和2号，而其他的很少被接受或不被接受，”布克纳维奇说。“您应该知道您的回收中心接受什么。”

以下是美国能源部提供的所有数字和字母的含义。

**最重要的符号**

“我认为指示容器使用范围的符号在 immediate 意义上最重要，”布克纳维奇说。“如果不遵守，可能会导致破裂，比如碎裂，这可能导致人身伤害。”

如果您想将容器放入微波炉、洗碗机或冷冻室，请确保存在相应的符号。忽略这些符号可能在短期内造成最多的问题。

虽然微波炉、洗碗机和冷冻室安全符号非常重要，必须遵守，但上面列出的所有符号都可以帮助您做出健康的选择。在选择和使用食品储存容器时，以下是一些需要记住的其他注意事项。

本文《这就是食品储存容器上的符号意味着什么》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
Sometimes I daydream about what it must have been like to go shopping when there was only one choice for everything. When you could just buy a shirt without wondering if it was moisture-wicking or hand-wash only, or eggs without concerns about whether the chickens were farm-raised or free-range. Honestly, that sounds delightful to me. But alas, and perhaps for the better, we now have much more information and many more choices when deciding which foods to eat and which products to use.

Take food-storage containers. Turn them around or flip them over and you’ll likely find a row of little symbols, all of which tell you something about the material used to make the container and may influence your decision about whether you want to use it. There are a lot of different symbols—some with obvious meanings and some that are a bit more ambiguous. And that’s putting it nicely.

So we’re here to clear it all up with a guide to what these symbols actually mean, courtesy of Martin Bucknavage, a retired food safety and quality specialist. Read on to get the lowdown—and learn why this info is more important than you may realize.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more home, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

How important is it to pay attention to a food-storage container’s symbols?

When your personal safety is involved (which it is in some cases), it’s pretty important to listen to what these symbols are telling you. Ignoring some of them could potentially render your food inedible or dangerous to eat, while ignoring others could mean you’re missing out on an opportunity to make a choice that would support the environment.

But here’s where it gets weird: Despite the potentially high personal and societal stakes, these symbols aren’t very consistent. Except for the recycling symbol, which was created in 1970 and is used universally, and the food-safe symbol, which was created by the European Union and is an international regulatory standard, none of the other symbols on food-storage containers are standardized. In fact, most were created or added by marketing departments so that customers would know how to best use the products. Symbols that mean the same thing also tend to resemble one another but have slight differences, making it hard to keep track of what’s what.

Of course, that’s where we come in.

What do the symbols on food-storage containers mean?

Here are the most common ones, including what they look like and what they mean. You’re welcome in advance!

Food-safe

What it looks like: A wine glass and a fork next to each other.

What it means: “It indicates that the material used in the product is considered safe for food contact,” says Bucknavage. That means two key things. First, that there aren’t any contaminants left over from the manufacturing process on the surface of the container. And second, if you’re using the container regularly as it’s meant to be used, the material itself won’t become a source of toxins that could be transmitted to your food.

“It’s useful when containers don’t immediately appear to be food containers,” Bucknavage adds.

BPA-free

What it looks like: Usually, this one will just be the words BPA Free. Sometimes, it will be BPA in a circle with a diagonal slash across it.

What it means: “This indicates that the container does not contain Bisphenol A (BPA), a chemical used to make certain types of food containers but has been found to leach into foods, where it can potentially become a health concern, especially for infants,” Bucknavage says.

According to the Mayo Clinic, BPA exposure may harm the brains of fetuses, infants and children, while separate research has suggested a link between BPA and high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes and heart disease. In other words, you probably don’t want it in the containers, dishes and cups you and your family use regularly.

Microwave-safe

What it looks like: Three horizontal wavy lines by themselves or inside a square meant to look like a microwave.

What it means: This one is pretty self-explanatory—it means you can safely put the container, with food in it, into the microwave for normal heating. “Not only should the container not melt or shatter, but there should not be chemical migration from the container itself,” Bucknavage says.

Freezer-safe

What it looks like: A snowflake inside a square, sometimes with a little drawing of a freezer.

What it means: The container will effectively protect food when put in the freezer. “The material is designed to withstand freezing temperatures without becoming brittle or cracking,” Bucknavage says.

Dishwasher-safe

What it looks like: A square with dishes at the bottom and water droplets coming down on them.

What it means: In short, that you can put the container in the dishwasher. To get a bit more technical, it means that the container can withstand the high temperatures of the dishwasher and any chemicals in your dish soap without warping or breaking.

Compostable

What it looks like: Leaves with the word compostable or BPI, or a compost pile that looks sort of like a cupcake with arrows around it.

What it means: “This indicates that the food-packaging material will break down into organic matter in a specific composting environment,” says Bucknavage. Per the U.S. Department of Agriculture, composting is the process of recycling organic materials into something that can be used to enrich soil and plants. If you compost at home, this symbol means you can add the container to your bin when you’re finished with it.

Biodegradable

What it looks like: A triangle made up of two arrows and one leaf, or this symbol inside a circle that says biodegradable or eco-friendly.

What it means: The material the container is made out of will eventually break down into simple, natural substances, explains Bucknavage. How is this different from the compostable designation? Something with a compostable symbol requires a composting environment, while the biodegradable symbol theoretically means the material will just break down on its own.

“The issue here is that there are questions about the time frame, the conditions in which the item will break down and whether any problematic materials are left,” Bucknavage says.

Recyclable

What it looks like: A triangle made of arrows with a number in the middle and a few letters beneath.

What it means: This symbol tells you if the type of plastic used in the container is able to be recycled. “Some are more apt to being recycled, like numbers 1 and 2, while others are rarely accepted or not accepted,” says Bucknavage. “You should be aware of what your recycling center accepts.”

Here’s what all the numbers and letters mean, per the U.S. Department of Energy.

  • 1 PET: Polyethylene terephthalate, a very common and easy-to-recycle plastic found in water and soda bottles as well as prepared- and frozen-food containers.
  • 2 HDPE: High-density polyethylene, another widely accepted plastic found in household cleaner bottles, cutting boards and some food containers.
  • 3 V: Polyvinyl chloride, PVC, which is found in toys and bottles and is harder to recycle.
  • 4 LDPE: Low-density polyethylene, a soft type of plastic used in thin plastic bags that’s typically accepted for recycling at grocery stores.
  • 5 PP: Polypropylene, found in straws, cups and food containers, and usually able to be recycled.
  • 6 PS: Polystyrene, aka Styrofoam, usually found in takeout containers and disposable cups, and generally not accepted in recycling programs.
  • 7 Other: This includes BPA, polycarbonate and bio-based plastics—all tricky to recycle.
  • 70 GL: Mixed glass, which can be recycled.
  • 71 GL: Clear glass, which can be recycled.
  • 72 GL: Green glass, which can be recycled.
  • 20 PAP: Made of at least 75% cardboard and can be recycled.
  • 21 PAP: Made of at least 75% plain paper and can be recycled.
  • 22 PAP: Made of at least 75% mixed paper and can be recycled.

Which is the most important symbol?

“I think the ones indicating where the container can be used are most important in an immediate sense,” says Bucknavage. “When not properly adhered to, it can result in breakage like shattering, which could result in physical injury.”

If you want to put your container in the microwave, dishwasher or freezer, make sure that the appropriate symbol is present. Ignoring these could cause the most problems in the short-term.

What else should you keep in mind about food-storage containers?

While the microwave-, dishwasher- and freezer-safe symbols are super important to abide by, all of the symbols outlined above can help you make healthy choices. Here are a few additional considerations to keep in mind when choosing and using food-storage containers.

  • Use the symbols to reduce waste. “Packaging waste is a huge issue,” says Bucknavage. “Whether it can be recycled or composted needs to be a concern for everyone.” Before even checking the symbols on your containers, make sure you know which plastics can be recycled in your town and, if you don’t compost at home, if there is a commercial composting center you can take those containers to. The environment will thank you.
  • Keep BPA away from babies. “BPA-free is especially a concern when we are using containers for infants and small children,” Bucknavage says. If you’re buying bottles, cups, dishes and food containers for little ones, choose BPA-free or glass whenever possible.
  • Consider ditching plastic altogether. “With concerns around microplastics getting into our foods, many are moving to glass or ceramic containers,” says Bucknavage. Indeed, scientific research has linked microplastics—teeny-tiny bits of plastic that can enter the body through ingestion, inhalation and even skin exposure—to a higher risk of heart attack and stroke. We’re still learning about how much damage microplastics can actually cause, but if you want to switch to glass or ceramic food-storage containers to limit your exposure, it’s definitely not a bad choice.

Sources:

  • Martin Bucknavage, food safety and quality specialist; email interview, July 2026
  • Mayo Clinic: “What is BPA? Should I be worried about it?”
  • U.S. Department of Agriculture: “Composting”
  • U.S. Department of Energy: “Consumer Guide to Recycling Codes”
  • The New England Journal of Medicine: “Microplastics and Nanoplastics in Atheromas and Cardiovascular Events”

What Those Numbers on Fruit Stickers Mean

What the Colored Dots on Food Packaging Mean

This Is What the Code on Your Milk Jug Means

The post This Is What the Symbols on Your Food-Storage Container Mean appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Where’s Your Third Space?
articles.content:
在复杂世界中的可信伙伴

我们将不再支持IE（Internet Explorer）。我们致力于为支持最新网络标准和安全规范的浏览器提供最佳网站体验，因此建议用户更新浏览器。
